tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Install Istio using istioctl and enable automatic sidecar injection. Label the default namespace with istio-injection, deploy a test application, and verify that each Pod has an Envoy sidecar container injected automatically.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Download and install Istio
curl -L https://istio.io/downloadIstio | sh -
cd istio-*/bin
export PATH=$PWD:$PATH

# Or via Helm:
# helm repo add istio https://istio-release.storage.googleapis.com/charts
# helm install istio-base istio/base -n istio-system --create-namespace
# helm install istiod istio/istiod -n istio-system

# 2. Install using the demo profile (Istio-recommended learning environment)
istioctl install --set profile=demo -y

# 3. Verify installation
kubectl get pods -n istio-system

# 4. Enable automatic sidecar injection (default namespace)
kubectl label namespace default ___=enabled

# 5. Deploy the test app (Bookinfo example)
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/bookinfo/platform/kube/bookinfo.yaml

# 6. Verify sidecar injection (READY column should show 2/2)
kubectl get pods
tasks.solution:
# 2. Install Istio
istioctl install --set profile=demo -y
# Istio core installed
# Istiod installed
# Egress gateways installed
# Ingress gateways installed

# 3. Verify installation
kubectl get pods -n istio-system
# NAME                                    READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# istio-egressgateway-xxx                 1/1     Running   0          2m
# istio-ingressgateway-xxx                1/1     Running   0          2m
# istiod-xxx                              1/1     Running   0          2m

# 4. Enable automatic sidecar injection
kubectl label namespace default istio-injection=enabled
# namespace/default labeled

# 5. Deploy Bookinfo
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/bookinfo/platform/kube/bookinfo.yaml

# 6. Verify sidecar injection
kubectl get pods
# NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# details-v1-xxx                2/2     Running   0          30s  <-- 2/2: app+sidecar
# productpage-v1-xxx            2/2     Running   0          30s
# ratings-v1-xxx                2/2     Running   0          30s
# reviews-v1-xxx                2/2     Running   0          30s
# reviews-v2-xxx                2/2     Running   0          30s
# reviews-v3-xxx                2/2     Running   0          30s

# List containers in a specific Pod (verify istio-proxy container)
kubectl describe pod productpage-v1-xxx | grep 'Container ID' -A3
tasks.explanation: Installing Istio creates istiod (the control plane) along with istio-ingressgateway and istio-egressgateway (data plane gateways). Adding the istio-injection=enabled label to a namespace causes all Pods created there afterward to have an Envoy proxy container (istio-proxy) injected automatically. READY 2/2 means both the application container and the Envoy sidecar are ready. Existing Pods are not auto-injected — use kubectl rollout restart to recreate them.
hints: If istioctl install is slow, install without --verify and then monitor readiness separately with kubectl get pods -n istio-system -w.
To inject the sidecar into Pods already running, restart all Deployments in the namespace: kubectl rollout restart deployment -n default
Use istioctl proxy-status to verify at a glance that all sidecar proxies are synchronized with istiod.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Use VirtualService and DestinationRule to split traffic across three versions of the Bookinfo reviews service (v1/v2/v3). Configure weight-based routing that sends 70% to v1 and 30% to v3. Validate the actual traffic distribution with repeated curl requests.
tasks.starter:
# 1. DestinationRule: define subsets (Pod groups by version)
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: DestinationRule
metadata:
  name: reviews-dr
spec:
  host: reviews
  subsets:
    - name: v1
      labels:
        version: ___
    - name: v2
      labels:
        version: ___
    - name: v3
      labels:
        version: ___

---
# 2. VirtualService: weight-based routing
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: reviews-vs
spec:
  hosts:
    - reviews
  http:
    - route:
        - destination:
            host: reviews
            subset: v1
          weight: ___    # v1 70%
        - destination:
            host: reviews
            subset: v3
          weight: ___    # v3 30%

# 3. Validate after applying (v1/v3 distinguished by ratings call pattern)
# v1: no star rating, v3: red star rating
for i in $(seq 1 10); do
  kubectl exec deploy/productpage-v1 -c istio-proxy -- \
    curl -s http://reviews:9080/reviews/1 | grep -o '"color":"[^"]*"'
done
tasks.solution:
# 1. Apply DestinationRule
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: DestinationRule
metadata:
  name: reviews-dr
spec:
  host: reviews
  subsets:
    - name: v1
      labels:
        version: v1
    - name: v2
      labels:
        version: v2
    - name: v3
      labels:
        version: v3
EOF

# 2. Apply VirtualService
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: reviews-vs
spec:
  hosts:
    - reviews
  http:
    - route:
        - destination:
            host: reviews
            subset: v1
          weight: 70
        - destination:
            host: reviews
            subset: v3
          weight: 30
EOF

# 3. Validate traffic distribution
# Call reviews 10 times from productpage Pod
for i in $(seq 1 10); do
  kubectl exec deploy/productpage-v1 -c istio-proxy -- \
    curl -s http://reviews:9080/reviews/1 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('reviews',[{}])[0].get('rating',{}).get('color','none'))"
done
# Expected: 'none' (v1, no rating) ~7 times, 'red' (v3, red stars) ~3 times

# Check VirtualService status
kubectl get virtualservice reviews-vs
kubectl get destinationrule reviews-dr
tasks.explanation: DestinationRule groups a service's Pods into subsets by version label. VirtualService then distributes traffic across those subsets using weights. The sum of weight values must equal 100. This mechanism is how Istio enables Canary deployments without Argo Rollouts. The Envoy sidecar intercepts all traffic and routes it according to the VirtualService rules, applying the traffic policy without any changes to application code.
hints: v1 has no rating feature and does not call ratings. v2 shows black stars; v3 shows red stars. The distribution can also be verified visually in the productpage UI.
If the sum of weights in a VirtualService is not 100, you will see the error: 'sum of weights must equal 100'.
Inspect the complete routing rules currently applied with kubectl get virtualservice -o yaml.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Apply PeerAuthentication to enforce mTLS in STRICT mode across the entire namespace. Verify that plain-text requests from a Pod without a sidecar are rejected after the change, and use istioctl to confirm the mTLS authentication state.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Check current mTLS state (PERMISSIVE by default)
istioctl x describe pod $(kubectl get pod -l app=productpage -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')

# 2. Apply STRICT mTLS
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default-mtls
  namespace: default
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: ___    # STRICT or PERMISSIVE

# 3. Re-check mTLS state
istioctl x describe pod $(kubectl get pod -l app=productpage -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')

# 4. Attempt access from a Pod without a sidecar (rejection expected)
kubectl run test-client --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -- \
  curl -v http://productpage:9080/productpage
# Pod without a sidecar → rejected by STRICT mTLS

# 5. Verify normal access via Bookinfo Ingress Gateway
kubectl get svc istio-ingressgateway -n istio-system
tasks.solution:
# 1. Check current state
istioctl x describe pod $(kubectl get pod -l app=productpage -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
# Effectively PERMISSIVE or inherits from mesh policy

# 2. Apply STRICT mTLS
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default-mtls
  namespace: default
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT
EOF

# 3. Re-check state
istioctl x describe pod $(kubectl get pod -l app=productpage -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
# mTLS: YES
# Effective PeerAuthentication: default-mtls

# 4. Attempt access from a Pod without a sidecar
# Run this Pod in a namespace without istio-injection to ensure no sidecar
kubectl run test-client -n kube-system --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -- \
  curl -v --max-time 5 http://productpage.default.svc.cluster.local:9080/productpage

kubectl logs test-client -n kube-system
# curl: (56) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
# or: (35) OpenSSL SSL_connect: Connection reset by peer
# STRICT mode rejects plaintext connections

# 5. Normal access via Bookinfo ingress gateway (gateway has a sidecar)
export INGRESS_PORT=$(kubectl -n istio-system get service istio-ingressgateway -o jsonpath='{.spec.ports[?(@.name=="http2")].nodePort}')
curl -s http://$(minikube ip):$INGRESS_PORT/productpage | head -20

# 6. mTLS validation tool
istioctl authn tls-check $(kubectl get pod -l app=productpage -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') reviews
# HOST:PORT            STATUS    SERVER    CLIENT    AUTHN POLICY        DESTINATION RULE
# reviews:9080         OK        STRICT    STRICT    default-mtls/...    reviews-dr/...
tasks.explanation: PeerAuthentication STRICT mode enforces mTLS for all inter-service communication in the namespace. Pods with Istio sidecars automatically negotiate an encrypted mTLS channel by exchanging certificates via Envoy. Plain-text traffic from Pods without sidecars is rejected in STRICT mode. PERMISSIVE mode allows both mTLS and plaintext for use during incremental migration. The istioctl authn tls-check command shows the actual mTLS negotiation result between two services.
hints: If existing inter-service communication breaks after applying STRICT mode, run istioctl x describe pod <name> to see which PeerAuthentication is in effect.
To exempt a specific service from STRICT mode, create a separate PeerAuthentication with spec.selector.matchLabels targeting only that Pod.
Istio automatically issues and rotates mTLS certificates based on SPIFFE/SPIRE every 24 hours. No manual certificate management is needed.

tasks.prompt: [Application] Install Istio's observability add-ons (Kiali, Jaeger) and experience service mesh visualization and distributed tracing. Generate traffic to Bookinfo, then view the service dependency graph in Kiali and inspect distributed trace spans for an HTTP request in Jaeger.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Install Kiali, Jaeger, Prometheus, and Grafana (Istio sample add-ons)
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/addons/kiali.yaml
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/addons/jaeger.yaml
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/addons/prometheus.yaml
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/addons/grafana.yaml

# 2. Wait for add-on Pods to be ready
kubectl get pods -n istio-system -w

# 3. Generate traffic (repeated requests to Bookinfo)
for i in $(seq 1 50); do
  curl -s -o /dev/null http://$(minikube ip):$INGRESS_PORT/productpage
done

# 4. Open Kiali dashboard
istioctl dashboard kiali
# Check service graph in the browser

# 5. Open Jaeger dashboard
istioctl dashboard jaeger
# Search for productpage service, then view traces
tasks.solution:
# 1. Install add-ons (run the commands above)

# 2. Wait for readiness (may take a few minutes)
kubectl get pods -n istio-system
# kiali-xxx             1/1     Running   0          2m
# jaeger-xxx            1/1     Running   0          2m
# prometheus-xxx        1/1     Running   0          2m
# grafana-xxx           1/1     Running   0          2m

# 3. Configure Ingress port and generate traffic
export INGRESS_PORT=$(kubectl -n istio-system get service istio-ingressgateway -o jsonpath='{.spec.ports[?(@.name=="http2")].nodePort}')
export INGRESS_HOST=$(minikube ip)

# Configure Bookinfo gateway (traffic entry point)
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/bookinfo/networking/bookinfo-gateway.yaml

# Generate repeated requests
for i in $(seq 1 50); do
  curl -s -o /dev/null http://$INGRESS_HOST:$INGRESS_PORT/productpage
done

# 4. Open Kiali (automatically opens browser)
istioctl dashboard kiali &
# Graph > Namespace: default
# View productpage → reviews(v1/v2/v3) → ratings dependency graph
# See traffic volume, error rates, and latency metrics visualized

# 5. Open Jaeger
istioctl dashboard jaeger &
# Service: productpage.default → Find Traces
# Click a trace to view the full request path (productpage→reviews→ratings) as spans

# 6. Grafana (Istio metrics dashboards)
istioctl dashboard grafana &
# Dashboards > Istio Service Dashboard

# 7. Clean up
kubectl delete -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.20/samples/bookinfo/platform/kube/bookinfo.yaml
kubectl delete peerauthentication default-mtls
kubectl delete virtualservice reviews-vs
kubectl delete destinationrule reviews-dr
tasks.explanation: Istio provides three observability capabilities without any application code changes. First, metrics (Prometheus/Grafana): request rate, error rate, and P99 latency are collected automatically. Second, distributed tracing (Jaeger/Zipkin): the latency of each service hop and the full request path are recorded as spans. Third, a service graph (Kiali): real-time traffic flows and service dependencies are visualized. Together these three form the three pillars of observability for a service mesh: metrics, traces, and logs.
hints: The istioctl dashboard command sets up kubectl port-forward automatically and opens a browser. To do it manually, run kubectl port-forward svc/kiali -n istio-system 20001:20001 and navigate to http://localhost:20001.

If traces are not appearing in Jaeger, check Envoy's sampling rate. The default is 1% (1 in 100 requests). To see more traces in a lab environment, raise the sampling rate to 100% using the Telemetry API.
If 'Unknown' services appear in Kiali, the corresponding Pods are missing app or version labels. Kiali uses these labels to draw the service graph.
